old_claim.note: Railroads provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: The factory-era urbanization process begins before the 1825 railway milestone; railways later accelerated urban growth but did not originate the scoped process.

source_shape.source_locator: The chapter treats 1750-1850 urbanization and industrialization as related processes, attributes much urban growth to migration, and notes the 1851 majority-urban census milestone.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Cambridge documents industrial urbanization as a migration-driven process extending from the eighteenth century into the mid-nineteenth century.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That chronology begins before railways and supports factories and migration as the initial process; later rail transport can accelerate growth without being its prerequisite.

ontology_before: Industrial urbanization inherited directly from railways despite the urbanization process beginning before the graph's first railway milestone.
ontology_after: Industrial urbanization retains factory and agricultural lineage while railways remain later transport infrastructure outside its origin edges.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: industrial_urbanization no longer directly depends on railroads.
would_reject_if: Direct demographic evidence showed that the scoped British industrial urbanization process began only after and because of railway deployment.
short_reason: Railways accelerated an urbanization process that was already underway.
